Voice

Text-to-speech responses powered by gTTS. The feature with a race condition in the audio player.

How it works

When you click the speaker icon on a response, the frontend sends the text to /api/tts. The backend uses gTTS to generate an MP3 file. The frontend plays the audio through an Audio element.

The audio player shows a loading state while the MP3 is being generated, a playing state while audio is playing, and an idle state when done.

The race condition

The Audio element fires an ended event when playback completes. But the ended event can fire before the audio is fully loaded, especially on slow connections or when the MP3 file is large.

The result: the player showed "done" while audio was still playing. The user could not replay because the button was already in the idle state.

The fix: add a loadeddata check. Only mark playback as complete after the ended event fires AND the audio has been fully loaded.

// Before: only checks ended event
audio.onended = () => { isPlaying = false; }

// After: checks both ended and loadeddata
audio.onended = () => {
    if (audio.readyState >= 2) {
        isPlaying = false;
    }
};
audio.onloadeddata = () => {
    audio.play();
};

gTTS limitations

gTTS uses Google Translate's TTS endpoint. It supports many languages but has rate limits. The first request after idle takes 1 to 2 seconds. Subsequent requests are faster because of connection keep-alive.

The audio quality is acceptable for a chat application but not for production voice applications. The MP3 files are typically 50 to 200 KB for a short response.

The macOS app

The Swift app does not have voice playback yet. The web frontend handles TTS through the Flask backend. The Swift app could use AVSpeechSynthesizer for native TTS or call the same backend endpoint.

Found something unexpected? Report an issue.
